coredata.dc:title: Voltage equalization circuit for retired batteries for energy storage applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: 2022 The 4th International Conference on Clean Energy and Electrical Systems
coredata.dc:creator: Habib, A.K.M. Ahasan
Hasan, Mohammad Kamrul
Islam, Shayla
Ahmed, Musse Mohamed
Aman, Azana Hafizah Mohd
Bagwari, Ashish
Khan, Sheroz
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, an active equalization circuit based on resonant converter is presented. This equalization circuit has been proposed to equalize the direct cell-to-cell voltage in a string. All electrochemical energy storage devices are connected in series. Using this equalization circuit energy transfer from higher energy and charge capacitive cell to lower energy and charge cell in the string. All MOSFET switches are operated by complementary Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) signal. The working principle of this equalization circuit like that of a switches-capacitor equalization circuit. In this circuit, a single Inductor (L) capacitor (C) energy carrier and bidirectional low voltage MOSFET switches are used so that it can recover maximum energy, reduce conduction loss, and improve the switching loss drawback, reduce the equalization time duration between two cells and achieved zero voltage gap. This equalization circuit is bidirectional and operates under the three modes, namely, charging, discharging, and relaxation mode. The proposed circuit details, the working principle, and the mathematical analyzes are presented. The simulation results are validated by the demonstration of an experimental prototype hardware testbed. The experimental results based on lithium-ion, lead–acid, and super-capacitor are presented. This equalization circuit is low cost and occupies little space with efficiencies of 96%, 94.2%, and 84.43% for Li-ion, lead–acid and super-capacitor respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage device
Cell equalization
Active equalization
Resonant converter
Electric vehicles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed equalization circuit prototype development in the laboratory for four ESD cells.
Proposed equalization circuit result (a) Two Li-ion battery (b) equalization test during discharging and charging period.
Proposed equalization circuit result (a) four lead–acid battery. (b) two-100F SC.
Cell equalization result comparison between conventional and proposed circuit.

Inductor (100 μ H)
Capacitor (200 μ C)

Li-ion battery (3.6 V, 4200 mAh, retired)
Lead-acid battery (12 V, 1.5 Ah, retired)
Super-capacitor (100 F, 2.7 V)

Medium ET, voltage gap between the cells

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2022 The 4th International Conference on Clean Energy and Electrical Systems (CEES 2022), 2–4 April 2022, Tokyo, Japan

head.ce:title: Voltage equalization circuit for retired batteries for energy storage applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, an active equalization circuit based on resonant converter is presented. This equalization circuit has been proposed to equalize the direct cell-to-cell voltage in a string. All electrochemical energy storage devices are connected in series. Using this equalization circuit energy transfer from higher energy and charge capacitive cell to lower energy and charge cell in the string. All MOSFET switches are operated by complementary Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) signal. The working principle of this equalization circuit like that of a switches-capacitor equalization circuit. In this circuit, a single Inductor (L) capacitor (C) energy carrier and bidirectional low voltage MOSFET switches are used so that it can recover maximum energy, reduce conduction loss, and improve the switching loss drawback, reduce the equalization time duration between two cells and achieved zero voltage gap. This equalization circuit is bidirectional and operates under the three modes, namely, charging, discharging, and relaxation mode. The proposed circuit details, the working principle, and the mathematical analyzes are presented. The simulation results are validated by the demonstration of an experimental prototype hardware testbed. The experimental results based on lithium-ion, lead–acid, and super-capacitor are presented. This equalization circuit is low cost and occupies little space with efficiencies of 96%, 94.2%, and 84.43% for Li-ion, lead–acid and super-capacitor respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The demand for Electric Vehicles (EV) and renewable energy have conspicuously increased over the years around the world due to fossil fuel depletion and concerns of global warming as well as environmental greenery foliage decline, offering plenty of emerging new fields of applications, such as electric vehicles (EVs), solar, and wind power systems. The energy storage device (ESD) cell, be they are electrochemical batteries or super-capacitors (SC), is the main source of power in portable electronic devices in consumers’ electronics. Such cells as strings form a battery package for applications in EV, industries, and play a critical role for energy storage applications in the domains of renewable energy (like as off-grid islanded micro-grid system, grid-connected micro-grids, satellite applications, etc.). Nowadays, EVs retired lithium-ion batteries deposits and recycling are becoming great challenges because of long life-span, which still have good storage capacity and safe operation. However, these retired batteries have lost 70%–80% of initial capacity and this storage/certain-residual capacity can used as ESD in renewable energy storage systems and off-grid islanded micro-grid systems. Though, currently retired battery application field is small but it will cover major renewable energy storage systems for safety, lower cost, high storage capacity, small size [1–3]. The limitation of manufacturing and that of the electrochemical digression of individual ESD cell voltage is varied due to which imbalance occurs during charge and discharge time in the string of cells of a battery package. Due to the imbalance, higher and lower capacitive ESD is not fully charged (or discharged) during the charging (or discharging period). If the charging or discharging procedure is continued in the string then ESD cells are prone to explosion causing safety concerns or get damaged affecting life span because of overcharge or under discharge [4].

To save the ESD cells from explosion or damage and to increase the lifetime, several equalization methods are presented. There are two groups of equalization circuits, namely: energy-consuming called passive equalization and energy recovery referred to as active equalization. In passive equalization, using the resistor for consuming the excess energy from the higher cell in the string to balance the lower cell. In active equalization, using capacitor, inductor and transformer to recover the energy from higher cell for passing it onto lower cell in the set of processes consisting of cell-to-cell (C2C), cell-to-pack (C2P), pack-to-cell (P2C), pack-to-pack (P2P) and cell-to-pack-to-cell (C2P2C) energy transitions [5–7].

Passive equalization circuits: In this equalization, the extra energy of the higher capacitive cells is not distributed. The extra energy from higher state of charge energy storage cell is diminished by the shunt resistor as heat aiming to match it to those with lower state of charge energy-storage cell on the string. Fixed shunt, switch shunt and analog shunt equalization are in this group. This equalization system is simple, easy to implement, low cost, less complex and efficient, cheap, and miniature size [5,8]. However, the passive methods of cell equalization cause a reduction in the available battery-pack capacity, requiring thus extra-heat management provisions.

C2C equalization: In the active C2C equalization methods is to actively transfer energy in between the cells through non-dissipative topologies. Here, the excess energy (and so charge) from the cells with higher energy is transferred to the cells with the lower one, balancing all to the same level. There active equalization circuits make use of the switches, capacitors, inductors, and transformer. This equalization system required smart voltage or charge sensing, many switches, a complex control system, good efficiency and suitable for low voltage application. Based on the energy transfer system on the cell string, this equalization system is divided into two groups: adjacent C2C equalization and direct C2C equalization [5,6,9].

Adjacent C2C equalization: In this equalization system, excessive energy is transferred between two adjacent cells on the string through inductor, capacitor, transformer, and converter. These equalization circuits are bidirectional and work on charging and discharge conditions. These topologies are switch capacitor [6], cuk converter [5,10], ramp converter [5] buck-boost converter [11], resonant converter [9,12,13], and full-bridge converter [5,9]. Among this converter, the ramp converter is unidirectional.

Direct C2C equalization: In this equalization system, excessive energy is transferred direct cell-to-cell on the string using inductor, capacitor, transformer, and converter. These equalization circuits use single capacitors [14,15], single inductors [5,9], single winding transformer [6,16], single resonant converter [17,18], double-tiered switched capacitors [5,19], and multi-inductors [9]. This equalization system requires accurate cell voltage sensing, complex control system.

C2P equalization: In C2P equalization system, excessive energy of from a single cell is transferred to the pack using inductor, transfer and converter. In this equalization system, a monitoring IC is continuously checking the cell status. When a cell voltage is higher than rest of the cell on the pack then it transfers its excessive energy. This equalization system, when transformer is used as an energy carrier then the excessive energy cell is connected in the primary side of the transformer. The C2P equalization system is using multi-winding transformer [20], multiple winding transformer [5,21], fly-back converter [22]. In this equalization, the control system requires the average nominal voltage of every cell in the pack and overall medium efficiency.

P2C equalization: In the P2C equalization system, when a cell faces low energy capacity and goes to under the minimum nominal voltage on the pack then energy transfer to the cell through of inductor, transformer, and converter. A monitoring IC continuously monitors the cell status. When a transformer is used as an energy carrier then all cell on the pack is individually connected on the primary side and lower cell connected on the secondary side. This topology is good efficient, fast equalization speed, and bidirectional. The equalization circuits are multiple winding transformer, a fly-back converter, multi winding transformer, single winding transformer [6].

C2P2C equalization: The C2P2C is the most complex equalization system. Here, energy transfer from C2C, C2P, P2C, and P2P through capacitor, inductor, transformer, and converter. These equalization topologies are single switch capacitor, single inductor, single winding transformer, switch capacitor, multi inductor, multi winding transformer, and flyback converter. In this equalization system double layer and coaxial multi winding, the transformer is used to transfer the energy [6,23]. The cell equalization time depends on the C2C, C2P, P2C, and P2P. However, the manufacturing and implementation of C2P2C are very complicated.

This paper presents a single direct cell-to-cell (C2C) LC energy carrier-based circuit used to balance a battery package of serially-connected ESD cells. The aim of the study is to reduce the equalization time between two cells in order to increase the efficiency of the equalization by reducing the number of associate components such as MOSFET switches, diode, etc. In the ESD cell string, when the storage energy variation occurs between two cells then the equalization process is activated to reinstate the variation. This circuit can be viable for P2P process and can be scaled up to any number of ESD cells in the direct C2C equalization process. This paper is structured so that Section 2 introduces the proposed circuit and working principle of the voltage equalization. The analysis of the two-cell voltage equalization process is detailed in Section 3, the Results and discussion are given in Section 4, and Section 5 has highlighted the quantitative conclusions.

Fig. 1 show the schematic diagram of the proposed circuit. This circuit compost part by part. Firstly, all MOSFET switches are connected with the ESD and single series LC energy carrier. Secondly, a monitoring IC connected with ESD and micro-controller to check the cell status. Thirdly, a master controller is connected with MOSFET switches (MS) through of MOSFET driver and monitor circuit. In these circuits, ESD1 and ESD
N
are coupled with two MS and

ESD2 to ESD
N−1
are coupled with bidirectional low voltage MS, to protect the negative voltage. Because of the single low voltage MOSFET, the intrinsic body diode cannot protect the negative voltage.

In this equalization circuit, the switching frequency was chosen as equal to LC resonant frequency so that soft switching can be achieved. All switches are operated the same gate plus near with ZCS (zero current switching) with 50% of the duty cycle. For this kind of switching LC energy carrier minimized the current impedance and minimized the equalization time by flowing the maximum current. When EDS cell voltage imbalanced arise in the string then controller detects the higher and lower cell and turn on the associate switches of the higher cell in positive half duty cycle to store the energy in LC energy carrier. After the negative half, the duty cycle associate switches of the lower cell is on and the LC energy carrier realized the energy to store in the lower cell. This process will continuously be repeated until the cell voltage is balance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two cell voltage equalization process analysis
ce:section.ce:para:

The equalization efficiency of this circuit is estimated by Eq. (9).

× i
× 100 %

The parasitic resistance of this circuit tries to keep low so that the maximum efficiency can achieve. The working principle of the equalization circuit as like the damped oscillation circuit and equalization time depends on the amplitude oscillation.

Using the PSIM simulation tool is conducted for verifying the proposed circuit. To validate the simulation, a prototype was developed in the laboratory (Fig. 2). LM301 Op-amp based differential amplifier (here, all resistance remains the same) is used for monitoring the cell status. All MOSFET switches gate is derived by photo-coupler, where complimentary PWM single used that generated from micro-controller. Two Li-ion battery based equalization results shown in Fig. 3(a) on the relaxation mode. Initially, each cell has 3.958 V and 3.712 V, where the voltage difference is 246 mV. To execute the equalization process, theoretically, cell balance will in 3.835 V but the equalization circuit achieved 0 mV after 83 min. The cell voltage becomes equal in 3.821 mV and using Eq. (9) equalization efficiency estimated near 96%. As the proposed equalization, the circuit is bidirectional and it works on charge and discharge mode so that a cyclic test conduct show in Fig. 3(b). Using no-load ET-PGM22A-24500, Eton DC motor for discharge, and 1 A current for the charge. Initially, the voltage difference was 89 mV, after 160 min voltage difference becomes 0 mV, and the next 40 min discharge together. Subsequently, the next 220 min cells are charged together and reached near a high voltage limit. Fig. 4(a) shows the four lead-acid-based equalization results in the series string in relaxation mode. In the beginning, individual cells voltage is 11.563 V, 11.457 V, 11.258 V, and 11.15 V. Initially, the voltage difference was 413 mV and after 207 min, cell voltage becomes 0 mV, and equalization efficiency is near 94.2%. Also, two 100F SC based on the experiment was conducted. Where the initial voltage was 2.674 V and 2.324 V. After 278 s, the voltage variation becomes 0 mV and achieves near 82.43% efficiency (shown in Fig. 4(b)). The significant inducement of this proposed circuit is to meet the 0 V gap, reduce the direct C2C equalization time, cost, and control complexity. This circuit single series LC is used for an energy carrier. Based on several key points, a comparison between the existing circuit and the proposed circuit is illustrated in Table 1.

The proposed circuit has allowed direct C2C equalization on the string, high equalization speed; minimize the power loss, highly efficient, miniature size, and low cost. Also, the result from the based comparison is illustrated in Fig. 5 and Table 2 so that the improvement of the proposed circuit is easily found out. For this comparison, we choose some research articles from 2016 to 2021. In our result section, we show the li-ion battery, lead acid battery and SC-based equalization result so that we chose similar types of research articles for comparison.

Yang et al. [2] proposed multi-inductor-based equalization circuit for retired batteries. They used 12 li-ion cell and that takes 103 min for cell voltage equalization but have voltage gaps (we used OCV carve for measure SOC to voltage). This circuit has some resistive loss in the power MOSFET. Compare with our circuit, we used a 4200 mAh li-ion cell and that takes 82 min for cell voltage equalization. Theoretically, our circuit equalizes the cell voltage in 3.85 V but it was equalized in 3.83 V.

Zeltser et al. [12] proposed resonant converter based equalization circuit. They used 12 V, 6.2Ah lead–acid battery. The initial voltage of these batteries are 12.48 V and 12.25 V. After 660 min this equalization does not equalize the battery voltage. Theoretically, this circuit equalizes the cell voltage in 12.36 V but it has 300 m V gaps. Compare with our circuit, we used four 12 V, 1.5 Ah lead acid batteries and it takes 210 min for cell voltage equalization. Theoretically, our circuit equalizes the cell voltage in 11.357 V but it was equalizing in 11.35 V.

Yu et al. [17] proposed single resonant converter based equalization circuit. They used 300F SC. The initial voltage of these SC are 2.5 V, 2.3 V and 2.0 V. After 15 min this equalization does not​ equalize the SC voltage. Theoretically, this circuit equalizes the cell voltage in 2.267 V but it has 10 mV gaps. Compare with our circuit, we used 100F SC and it takes 4.64 min for SC voltage equalization and achieved 0 V gaps.

This paper proposed a single resonant converter based equalization circuit over the existing circuits. This equalization circuit is bidirectional and performs on charging, discharging, and relaxation mode. This presented circuit, any number of ESD cells, can be scaled up to balance the cell voltage. This circuit also applicable to the P2P equalization system. Additionally, this circuit has reduced the equalization time (for two 4200 mAh, 3.7 V Li-ion cells, it takes 76 min, 207 min for four 12 V, 1.5 Ah lead acid batteries and 4.64 min for 100 F SC), high efficiency (96% for Li-ion battery, 94.2 for lead–acid battery and 83.6 for SC respectively), zero voltage gap, minimum cost, and miniature size. This is because the number of switches and diodes have reduced, Op-amp based cell monitoring circuit, and other associate components. All bi-directional switches are consisting of very low resistance MOSFET for this equalization time is reduced, and efficiency increased. In studies, temperature variation did not consider that will be consider and present in future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) under the GGPM 2020-028, and GP-2021-K023208.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Ilya Zeltser, Michael Evzelman, Alon Kuperman, Mor Mordechai Peretz, Zero current switching resonant converter based parallel balancing of serially connected batteries string, IEEE Trans Ind Appl 55 (6) 7452–7460, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 2016 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: (a) Zeltser, Ilya, Michael Evzelman, Alon Kuperman, and Mor Mordechai Peretz.
